description: Cost-optimization epic, candidate #6 (owner-directed 2026-07-08, 'linearly decomposed all-Haiku' single-session reading). IDENTICAL to `haiku-swap` in model (all Haiku), system prompt, skill, and tools -- the ONLY changed dimension is the task prompt, which converts the implicit diligence obligations Haiku demonstrably skips into EXPLICIT, mechanically-verifiable checklist steps. Motivation (2026-07-07 investigation + the haiku-swap-verified coordinator race): Haiku executes explicit mechanical steps flawlessly (priors fetch/save, cat steps) but skips implicit obligations -- it saved all 3 prior briefs and never read them (dedup 2 while Sonnet, which grepped the priors story-by-story, scored 4), skipped follow-up labelling, and under-searched (5 vs 17). The hardened prompt adds three forcing functions: (1) a MANDATORY 'cat each prior brief now' step BEFORE research so priors are in context; (2) an explicit labelling clause during writing (any story continuing prior coverage MUST carry a follow-up label naming the prior day); (3) a pre-finish SELF-CHECK step -- verify all five output files exist, re-read the brief against each prior and fix any missing labels, and write /workspace/overlap-notes.md as an auditable record of every overlap and its labelling status (an extra captured artifact). Hypothesis: checklist decomposition alone recovers Haiku's dedup diligence (2 -> ~4) at essentially haiku-swap's price (~$0.40), isolating the cheapest lever (prompt forcing) from the structural lever (multi-agent). Content-selection quality is NOT expected to improve (editorial judgment is intelligence-bound, not diligence-bound). Content generation ONLY: holds no AWS/delivery role (FR-1), never fans out, never synthesizes audio; only delivery-side contact is the read-only GET /recent-briefs route. SEPARATE agent resource; syncing/triggering it can never mutate production or any other candidate.
schedule_intent: none -- cost-optimization eval candidate only; never scheduled and never promoted to production without a separate, explicitly-approved cut-over decision
